answers.value: Medical billing specialists and coding staff work in closely spaced cubicles (approximately 4–5 feet apart) processing patient accounts, posting payments, and resolving insurance denials. Staff spend most of the day seated at desktop computers using Epic and payer portals, frequently standing and leaning into neighboring cubicles to review complex claims together, share dual-monitor screens, or provide on-the-job training. Supervisors walk between rows of cubicles to answer questions, which often results in brief huddles of 3–4 employees around one workstation within a confined aisle. Telephone calls to insurance companies and patients are made from each workstation; employees sometimes lower masks to be more clearly understood on long calls, especially when dealing with noisy printer activity nearby. Break schedules are staggered but not perfectly adhered to, leading to overlapping presence at desks and shared use of adjacent cubicles during shift changes. Confidential paper documents are passed hand-to-hand, and staff often roll their chairs into the aisle to collaborate, making sustained 6-foot distancing impractical during peak hours (9:00 a.m.–3:00 p.m.).
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: HIM clerks are assigned to a compact scanning room with four large high-speed scanners and three indexing workstations arranged along two opposing walls, with only narrow walking space between them (approximately 3 feet). Workers receive physical charts and loose documents in shared bins, prepare and scan records, perform quality checks on digital images, and then file or shred originals. Because the scanners are loud and jam-prone, employees frequently stand shoulder-to-shoulder to clear jams, adjust settings, and verify that sensitive documents are captured correctly. Training of new staff occurs directly at a single scanner, with the trainer and trainee standing within 1–2 feet of each other for extended periods to walk through step-by-step procedures. Staff often turn their monitors toward coworkers to ask about ambiguous handwriting or missing signatures, pulling others into very close proximity. Due to limited floor space, carts of records are parked in the middle of the room, further reducing available distance and forcing people to squeeze past one another multiple times per hour. Ventilation is limited and doors are generally kept closed for privacy and noise control, contributing to prolonged shared air exposure when multiple staff are present.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Patient access representatives are stationed at a continuous front desk counter that is divided into six registration stations, each separated only by a low partial divider that does not extend fully between workers. Representatives sit side-by-side, typically less than 4 feet apart, checking in and registering a high volume of patients and family members throughout the day. Staff collect identification, insurance cards, and co-pays, and assist with electronic signature pads that are shared between patients and employees. While there are plexiglass barriers between staff and patients, there are no full-height partitions between staff members themselves, so representatives frequently lean toward one another to clarify scheduling rules, insurance coverage, or to review complex registration scenarios. During peak times (early morning and late afternoon clinic rush), additional staff float behind the seated registrars, hovering close to their chairs to assist, troubleshoot technical issues, or override system errors. This often creates clusters of 3–4 employees within a small area, with less than 3 feet between individuals. The desk area is also adjacent to a narrow corridor leading to the imaging suite, causing additional close contact as clinical staff and escorts pass directly behind the seated registrars.

answers.value: IT support technicians share a rectangular workroom with a central table used for hardware repairs and four desks positioned along the walls. Because of the need to collaborate on complex tickets and troubleshoot devices, technicians frequently gather around a single workstation or laptop, leaning in closely to view error messages or configuration screens. The central table is used to stage and repair equipment such as thin clients, label printers, and barcode scanners; two or three staff members will often work on different devices at the same table, less than 3 feet apart, while discussing issues and sharing tools. When rolling out software updates or resolving system outages, one technician screenshares while others crowd around to observe and provide input, remaining in close proximity for 30–60 minutes at a time. New hires are trained in person with side-by-side shadowing at the main help desk station, including live phone support with staff from other departments, requiring the trainee and trainer to share one headset or sit closely enough for both to hear the call. Boxes of equipment are stacked along the walls and in corners, limiting walking space and resulting in frequent shoulder-to-shoulder passing when moving around the room. Because calls can be difficult to hear over equipment noise, staff sometimes remove or adjust masks while speaking on speakerphones, especially when multiple people are participating in troubleshooting.

answers.value: Although this is a non-patient area, lab support staff and couriers work in a tight processing office receiving, sorting, and logging specimens that have already been collected in clinical areas. Ten employees share a U-shaped counter with computer terminals spaced 3–4 feet apart, centrifuges, label printers, and racks for specimen tubes. During peak morning and early afternoon hours, multiple couriers arrive within minutes of each other, congregating at the same counter space to hand off specimens, sign chain-of-custody logs, and clarify routing questions. Staff frequently reach across each other to access shared label printers, barcode scanners, and supply drawers, resulting in sustained close contact. When resolving mislabeled or incomplete orders, two or three staff members gather around a single monitor to cross-check orders in the LIS and EHR, often staying within 2–3 feet for extended problem-solving. New employees are mentored at the bench with direct side-by-side instruction on sorting, aliquoting, and preparing specimens for transport to the main lab; this training can last several hours at a time. The office footprint is small relative to the volume of staff and equipment, and the path between the entrance and the back bench is narrow, forcing people to squeeze past each other frequently. While this area does not involve direct patient care, the density of staff, shared equipment, and continuous movement make consistent 6-foot physical distancing unachievable.

Workstations are fixed and assigned by role, with each cubicle occupied daily. During month-end close and large payer audit periods, additional temporary staff or auditors (1–2 people) may be brought in and seated at spare desks located within the same cubicle cluster, further increasing density.

answers.value: Key job tasks in this area include: (1) Reviewing electronic health records (EHR) and coding documentation to ensure accurate assignment of CPT, ICD-10, and HCPCS codes; (2) Entering and updating insurance information, including coordination of benefits, prior authorizations, and referral data; (3) Placing and receiving high-volume phone calls to insurance companies and patients to resolve claim denials, request additional information, and set up payment plans; (4) Conducting side-by-side peer reviews of complex accounts, where a billing specialist and coder sit or stand closely together to walk through documentation line by line; (5) Participating in impromptu cube-side huddles when payer rules or internal processes change, which results in 4–6 workers gathering in one narrow aisle to discuss workflows; (6) Sharing printers, fax machines, and shredders located in common areas at the ends of cubicle rows, leading to frequent close contact as staff wait in line or reach past each other. Because the work is deadline-driven and highly collaborative, employees often choose proximity over distancing to resolve issues quickly, for example by rolling their chairs into the aisle and physically pointing at each other’s monitors. New employees are trained through direct over-the-shoulder observation, with the trainer narrating each step while the trainee mirrors the actions, typically lasting 1–2 hours at a stretch. While masks and hand hygiene are encouraged, the layout and nature of the work make consistent 6-foot distancing unrealistic, particularly during time-sensitive projects and month-end close activities.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Primary tasks in the HIM scanning room include: (1) Physical chart preparation, where clerks remove staples, sort documents, and affix barcoded coversheets, often working side-by-side at a single preparation table; (2) Operating high-speed scanners that require frequent monitoring, clearing of paper jams, and fine adjustments to feed trays and sensors, which often brings two employees shoulder-to-shoulder at the machine; (3) Performing image quality control on dual-monitor workstations, with staff zooming in on signatures and handwriting while conferring with coworkers who stand very close to view the same screen; (4) Indexing scanned images into the correct patient charts in the EHR, which involves verifying names, dates of birth, and encounter numbers and sometimes asking a nearby coworker to double-check ambiguous data; (5) Handling and transporting stacks of charts and document bins, which narrows walking paths and results in employees squeezing past each other multiple times per hour; (6) One-on-one mentoring of new clerks, where the lead clerk stands directly beside the trainee at the scanner or workstation, sometimes sharing a mouse or keyboard to demonstrate specific steps. Because the room is relatively small and filled with bulky equipment, there is limited ability to rearrange workstations for distancing, and staff naturally cluster around whichever scanner is functioning best or processing the current priority workload.

answers.value: Job tasks at the front desk include: (1) Greeting patients and visitors upon arrival, verifying appointments, and directing them to the correct service areas; (2) Collecting demographic, insurance, and emergency contact information, which involves extended face-to-face interactions with patients and occasional interpretation support for non-English speakers; (3) Handling payment transactions for co-pays and prior balances, including passing credit card terminals, pens, and paper receipts back and forth across the counter; (4) Assisting patients with completion of electronic forms on shared tablets or signature pads, often requiring the registrar to lean around the plexiglass barrier to point out specific fields or buttons; (5) Providing on-the-job training to new registrars in a side-by-side fashion at a single workstation, with both individuals sharing the same keyboard, mouse, and phone handset during live registrations; (6) Collaborating with nearby coworkers on complex insurance questions by turning monitors for others to view, standing close together to review coverage screens, and raising voices to be heard over lobby noise. During busy clinic start times, registrars frequently call over supervisors or financial counselors to help with escalated issues, resulting in small clusters of staff standing within a few feet of each other behind the desk. Because the registration counter is fixed and space behind it is limited, there is no practical way to maintain 6-foot separation when more than a few employees are present.

answers.value: The IT help desk team performs: (1) Real-time phone and ticket-based technical support for staff across the organization, which requires technicians to consult one another frequently on unusual or high-impact issues; (2) Side-by-side troubleshooting of hardware such as thin clients, desktop PCs, printers, and mobile devices on a central worktable, where two or three staff may cluster around a single device to inspect components or cables; (3) Collaborative problem-solving sessions at one computer, where an experienced analyst walks a less experienced technician through advanced configuration steps while both focus on the same screen; (4) Hands-on equipment imaging and software installation, involving shared use of USB drives, keyboards, and headsets, often passed directly between workers; (5) In-person training of the IT intern, which usually consists of shadowing at a primary workstation, with the intern sitting or standing within arm’s reach of the trainer while they handle live calls and remote sessions; (6) Group response to outages, where all available technicians gather in the room to monitor dashboards, join multi-party conference calls on speakerphone, and coordinate response steps, standing or sitting close together due to limited space. While some work can be done remotely, the need for physical access to devices and direct collaboration in this room means that staff spend prolonged periods in close proximity, especially during critical incidents or large-scale deployments.

answers.value: Typical tasks in this office include: (1) Receiving incoming specimen bags and containers from couriers and clinical staff, which often results in multiple people standing at the same counter space to open bags, verify orders, and sign logs; (2) Sorting and organizing tubes and containers by test type and destination, with staff working side-by-side along the same bench and frequently reaching across one another for racks and labels; (3) Printing and applying barcode labels using shared printers and scanners, where several workers may line up or cluster around a single device to process high-volume batches; (4) Preparing specimens for analysis, including aliquoting, centrifuging, and placing tubes into transport racks, which is often taught and performed in a close, hands-on manner to ensure correct technique; (5) Resolving order discrepancies and missing information by gathering around a single computer to cross-check the EHR and laboratory information system, with two or three staff leaning in simultaneously to read the same screen; (6) Coordinating courier routes and timing, which sometimes leads to small group discussions near the doorway or central counter, limiting the ability of others to pass through without coming within arm’s length. Because the room is densely furnished with counters, equipment, and storage cabinets, walking paths are narrow and shared work surfaces are unavoidable, making sustained 6-foot distancing impractical during normal operations.
